content.text: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that lets an application obtain limited access to a resource (such as a user's files or email) on behalf of a user, without the application ever seeing the user's password. The user authenticates directly with the identity provider (Microsoft Entra ID), which issues short-lived access tokens scoped to specific permissions.

Entra ID implements OAuth 2.0 as its authorization layer and OpenID Connect (OIDC) on top for authentication. Every call to Microsoft Graph, Azure Resource Manager, and custom APIs protected by Entra ID uses OAuth 2.0 access tokens.

attrs.alt: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow with PKCE showing the 11-step sequence between user, client app, Entra ID, and resource server

content.text: OAuth 2.0 defines several grant types (flows) for different application scenarios. Entra ID supports four main grants. Choosing the wrong grant type is a common security mistake — always match the grant to the application type.
User signs in and app acts on their behalf
Device has no browser for redirect
API calls downstream API with same user context

The implicit grant (token returned directly from /authorize) is deprecated by Microsoft. All new apps must use Authorization Code + PKCE instead, which is safe for SPAs and mobile apps without a client secret.

The Authorization Code flow with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) is the recommended flow for all apps where a user signs in. PKCE eliminates the need for a client secret by proving the app that initiated the authorization request is the same one exchanging the code for tokens. The client generates a random code_verifier, hashes it into a code_challenge, and sends the challenge up front. Later, it proves ownership by sending the raw verifier.

import hashlib, base64, os, urllib.parse, requests

TENANT_ID    = "<your-tenant-id>"
CLIENT_ID    = "<your-app-client-id>"
REDIRECT_URI = "http://localhost:8080/callback"
SCOPES       = "openid profile User.Read offline_access"

# Step 1: Generate PKCE code_verifier and code_challenge
code_verifier  = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(os.urandom(32)).rstrip(b'=').decode()
code_challenge = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(
    hashlib.sha256(code_verifier.encode()).digest()
).rstrip(b'=').decode()

# Step 2: Build the authorization URL
params = {
    'client_id':             CLIENT_ID,
    'response_type':         'code',
    'redirect_uri':          REDIRECT_URI,
    'scope':                 SCOPES,
    'response_mode':         'query',
    'code_challenge':        code_challenge,
    'code_challenge_method': 'S256',
    'state':                 base64.urlsafe_b64encode(os.urandom(16)).decode(),
}
auth_url = (
    f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/authorize?'
    + urllib.parse.urlencode(params)
)
print('Open this URL in a browser:', auth_url)

# Step 3: After redirect, exchange the code for tokens
auth_code = input('Paste the code from the redirect URL: ').strip()

token_resp = requests.post(
    f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
    data={
        'client_id':     CLIENT_ID,
        'grant_type':    'authorization_code',
        'code':          auth_code,
        'redirect_uri':  REDIRECT_URI,
        'code_verifier': code_verifier,  # PKCE proof
        'scope':         SCOPES,
    }
)
tokens = token_resp.json()
print('access_token:', tokens.get('access_token', '')[:40], '...')
print('expires_in:', tokens.get('expires_in'), 'seconds')
print('scope granted:', tokens.get('scope'))
Never store tokens in localStorage — use sessionStorage or an in-memory variable for SPAs to mitigate XSS token theft
The state parameter prevents CSRF: generate a random value, store in session, verify it matches on the redirect
For web apps with a server-side component, send the code to your backend for the token exchange — keeps tokens off the browser entirely
The redirect_uri must exactly match one registered in the Entra app registration (including trailing slash)
Client Credentials Flow for Daemon Applications

The Client Credentials grant is used by background services, pipelines, and daemons that run without a signed-in user. The application authenticates with its own identity (client_id + client_secret or certificate) and receives an app-only token. These tokens contain no user claims — the `sub` and `oid` fields identify the application, not a person.

import requests

TENANT_ID     = "<your-tenant-id>"
CLIENT_ID     = "<daemon-app-client-id>"
CLIENT_SECRET = "<app-secret>"  # or use certificate — prefer cert in prod

# Request a token with the .default scope (all app permissions granted in Entra)
resp = requests.post(
    f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
    data={
        'client_id':     CLIENT_ID,
        'client_secret': CLIENT_SECRET,
        'scope':         'https://graph.microsoft.com/.default',
        'grant_type':    'client_credentials',
    }
)
token = resp.json()['access_token']

# Call Microsoft Graph with the app token
users = requests.get(
    'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users',
    headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'}
).json()
print(f'Found {len(users["value"])} users')

# NOTE: Client credentials tokens are app-only (no user context).
# The audience ('aud') is 'https://graph.microsoft.com' and there is no 'oid' sub for a user.
# Use for background services, pipelines, and automation — never for apps acting on behalf of a user.
Call APIs; passed as Bearer header
Obtain new access tokens without re-authentication
Identify the signed-in user in the app

Prefer certificate credentials over client secrets for daemon apps in production. Certificates are rotatable independently of secrets, can be stored in Azure Key Vault, and can be audited in Entra sign-in logs by certificate thumbprint.

Access tokens expire after 1 hour by default. Rather than forcing the user to sign in again, the application uses a refresh token to silently obtain a new access token. Refresh tokens have a 90-day sliding-window lifetime in Entra ID — as long as they are used at least once every 90 days, they remain valid. Password changes and admin revocation invalidate them immediately.

import requests

TENANT_ID     = "<your-tenant-id>"
CLIENT_ID     = "<your-app-client-id>"
REFRESH_TOKEN = "<stored-refresh-token>"  # persisted from prior token response

resp = requests.post(
    f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
    data={
        'client_id':     CLIENT_ID,
        'grant_type':    'refresh_token',
        'refresh_token': REFRESH_TOKEN,
        'scope':         'openid profile User.Read offline_access',
    }
)
new_tokens = resp.json()
new_access  = new_tokens['access_token']
new_refresh = new_tokens.get('refresh_token', REFRESH_TOKEN)  # Entra may rotate it

# Refresh tokens from Entra ID have a sliding window:
# - Default lifetime: 90 days, reset on each successful use
# - Maximum inactive lifetime: 90 days
# - Confidential clients (with client_secret): up to 1 year
# Tokens are invalidated immediately when the user changes password or revokes sessions.
print('New access token obtained, expires in:', new_tokens.get('expires_in'), 'seconds')
Cache access tokens in memory and only call /token when the token is within 5 minutes of expiry
Store refresh tokens encrypted at rest — they are long-lived credentials that can be used to impersonate the user
MSAL (Microsoft Authentication Library) handles token caching and refresh automatically; prefer it over raw HTTP calls
Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) can revoke access tokens early (before 1h) when Entra detects a risk event

When your API receives a Bearer access token, it must validate the token before trusting the claims. Validation involves: verifying the JWT signature against Entra's published public keys, checking the expiry (`exp`) and not-before (`nbf`) claims, confirming the audience (`aud`) matches your API's App ID URI, and verifying the issuer (`iss`) matches your tenant.

import jwt, requests
from jwt.algorithms import RSAAlgorithm

# Microsoft publishes public signing keys at the OIDC discovery endpoint
TENANT_ID = "<your-tenant-id>"
OIDC_CONFIG_URL = f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration'

config = requests.get(OIDC_CONFIG_URL).json()
jwks_uri = config['jwks_uri']  # e.g. https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/discovery/v2.0/keys
jwks = requests.get(jwks_uri).json()

ACCESS_TOKEN = "<jwt-access-token-to-validate>"

# Decode header to get kid (key ID)
header = jwt.get_unverified_header(ACCESS_TOKEN)
matching_key = next((k for k in jwks['keys'] if k['kid'] == header['kid']), None)
public_key = RSAAlgorithm.from_jwk(matching_key)

# Validate: signature, expiry, issuer, audience
claims = jwt.decode(
    ACCESS_TOKEN,
    public_key,
    algorithms=['RS256'],
    audience='<your-api-app-id-uri>',  # e.g. api://your-client-id
    issuer=f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/v2.0',
)
print('Subject (user):', claims.get('oid'))    # object ID in Entra
print('App ID (client):', claims.get('azp'))  # client_id of the calling app
print('Roles:', claims.get('roles', []))       # app roles assigned to the user
print('Scopes:', claims.get('scp', '').split())  # delegated scopes

# In production: cache the JWKS (refresh only when kid is unknown) — Entra rotates keys periodically
Never trust a token without validating the signature — a forged token with any claim would be accepted otherwise
Cache the JWKS response and only re-fetch when you encounter a `kid` not in your cache — Entra rotates keys without notice
Check `scp` (delegated scopes) for user-context tokens and `roles` (app roles) for app-only tokens — both can appear in the same token
For multi-tenant apps, validate `iss` against the issuer template `https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenantId}/v2.0` and verify `tid` is an allowed tenant
Libraries like `microsoft-identity-web` (.NET) and `msal-node` handle validation automatically — use them in production
On-Behalf-Of Flow for Chained API Calls

When a middle-tier API (your service) needs to call a downstream API (such as Microsoft Graph) with the original user's identity, it uses the On-Behalf-Of (OBO) grant. The middle-tier API exchanges the access token it received from the client for a new token scoped to the downstream API — preserving the user context throughout the call chain.

OBO is the correct pattern for microservice architectures where multiple layers of APIs need to act on behalf of a user. The alternative — storing the user's credentials or using client credentials — would lose the user context and break least-privilege authorization.

# On-Behalf-Of flow: middle-tier API calls a downstream API with the user's identity
# Scenario: Client app -> API A (your service) -> Microsoft Graph (downstream API)
# API A receives a user-delegated token, then exchanges it for a Graph token

import requests

TENANT_ID     = "<your-tenant-id>"
CLIENT_ID     = "<api-a-client-id>"      # your middle-tier API's app registration
CLIENT_SECRET = "<api-a-client-secret>"  # or certificate

def call_on_behalf_of(user_access_token: str) -> dict:
    """Exchange user token for a downstream Microsoft Graph token."""
    resp = requests.post(
        f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
        data={
            'grant_type':            'urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer',
            'client_id':             CLIENT_ID,
            'client_secret':         CLIENT_SECRET,
            'assertion':             user_access_token,   # the token the client sent to API A
            'requested_token_use':   'on_behalf_of',
            'scope':                 'https://graph.microsoft.com/User.Read Mail.Read',
        }
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()  # contains access_token scoped to Graph

# In a FastAPI handler:
# @app.get('/api/profile')
# async def get_profile(authorization: str = Header(...)):
#     user_token = authorization.removeprefix('Bearer ')
#     graph_token = call_on_behalf_of(user_token)['access_token']
#     profile = requests.get('https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me',
#         headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {graph_token}'}).json()
#     return profile

# Requirements:
# 1. API A must have the downstream API's scopes granted (admin consent)
# 2. The user must have consented to those scopes when signing in to the client app
# 3. The incoming token's 'aud' must match API A (not Graph directly)
The incoming token's 'aud' must equal your API's App ID URI — if clients call Graph directly with their token and pass it to your API, OBO fails
Both the client app and your API must have admin consent for the downstream scopes
OBO tokens are user-delegated — they carry the user's oid and scp claims in the resulting Graph token
Use MSAL's acquire_token_on_behalf_of() method rather than raw HTTP — it handles token caching and avoids redundant /token calls

OAuth 2.0 scopes restrict what the token permits. In Entra ID, scopes map to permissions defined in an app's manifest. There are two permission types: Delegated permissions (act on behalf of a signed-in user, appear in `scp` claim) and Application permissions (app-only, no user, appear in `roles` claim). Application permissions always require admin consent.

Consent can be granted by a user (for delegated permissions) or by an admin (for any permission). Admin consent is required when: the permission is an Application permission, the tenant has disabled user consent, or the scope is tagged as 'admin-only' by the API publisher.

# App Registration: configure API permissions (delegated + application)
# Using Microsoft Graph PowerShell

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Application.ReadWrite.All", "AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All"

# Get your app registration
$app = Get-MgApplication -Filter "displayName eq 'My API App'"

# Add delegated permission: User.Read on Microsoft Graph
$graphSp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'"
$userReadScope = $graphSp.Oauth2PermissionScopes | Where-Object Value -eq 'User.Read'

Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId $app.Id -RequiredResourceAccess @(
    @{
        resourceAppId = "00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000"  # Microsoft Graph
        resourceAccess = @(
            @{ id = $userReadScope.Id; type = "Scope" }     # delegated (user context)
        )
    }
)

# Grant admin consent for all users in the tenant (for delegated permissions):
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$($app.AppId)'"
New-MgServicePrincipalOauth2PermissionGrant -ServicePrincipalId $sp.Id \
    -ResourceId $graphSp.Id \
    -Scope "User.Read Mail.Read" \
    -ConsentType "AllPrincipals"  # tenant-wide consent

# Grant application permissions (for client credentials / app-only):
$userReadAllRole = $graphSp.AppRoles | Where-Object Value -eq 'User.Read.All'
New-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment \
    -ServicePrincipalId $sp.Id \
    -ResourceId $graphSp.Id \
    -AppRoleId $userReadAllRole.Id \
    -PrincipalId $sp.Id
# Note: application permissions always require admin consent — users cannot grant them

Request the minimum scopes your app needs. Users and admins see the consent prompt and may deny an app that requests excessive permissions. Over-scoped apps also increase the blast radius if a token is compromised.
Device Code Flow for CLIs and Headless Apps

The Device Code flow is designed for applications that cannot launch a browser: command-line tools, scripts running on servers, IoT devices, and smart TVs. The application gets a short code and a URL; the user opens the URL on any browser, enters the code, signs in, and the app polls for the token. No redirect URI is needed.

# Device Code flow: for CLIs, scripts, and devices without a browser
import requests, time

TENANT_ID = "<your-tenant-id>"
CLIENT_ID = "<public-client-app-id>"  # must be registered as a public client
SCOPES    = "openid profile User.Read"

# Step 1: Request a device code
device_resp = requests.post(
    f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/devicecode',
    data={'client_id': CLIENT_ID, 'scope': SCOPES}
).json()

print(device_resp['message'])
# Output: 'To sign in, use a web browser to open the page https://microsoft.com/devicelogin
#          and enter the code ABCD-EFGH to authenticate.'

# Step 2: Poll /token until user completes sign-in
interval = device_resp.get('interval', 5)
device_code = device_resp['device_code']

while True:
    time.sleep(interval)
    token_resp = requests.post(
        f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
        data={
            'client_id':   CLIENT_ID,
            'grant_type':  'urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code',
            'device_code': device_code,
        }
    ).json()

    if 'access_token' in token_resp:
        print('Signed in! Access token:', token_resp['access_token'][:40], '...')
        break
    elif token_resp.get('error') == 'authorization_pending':
        continue  # user hasn't finished signing in yet
    elif token_resp.get('error') == 'expired_token':
        print('Device code expired (15 minutes). Restart the flow.')
        break
    else:
        print('Error:', token_resp)
        break
Device codes expire after 15 minutes — if the user hasn't signed in by then, restart the flow
The polling interval is returned in the device code response — do not poll faster to avoid 'slow_down' errors
Register the app as a public client (no secret) since the app runs on untrusted devices
Device Code flow supports MFA — the user completes MFA in the browser during the sign-in step

OAuth 2.0 flows fail for predictable reasons: misconfigured redirect URIs, missing consent, wrong scopes, and expired secrets. Understanding the AADSTS error codes returned by Entra ID's /token endpoint speeds up debugging significantly.

# Common Entra ID OAuth 2.0 errors and their causes

# AADSTS65001: 'The user or administrator has not consented to use the application'
# Fix: Add required API permissions in the app registration and grant admin consent, OR
#      include the scope in the authorization request so the user is prompted to consent

# AADSTS70011: 'The provided value for the input parameter scope is not valid'
# Fix: Scope must match an API permission declared in the app registration.
#      Use 'https://graph.microsoft.com/User.Read' not just 'User.Read' for Graph.

# AADSTS7000215: 'Invalid client secret provided'
# Fix: The secret in your code doesn't match the app registration. Regenerate in Entra,
#      update the secret in Key Vault / environment variable.

# AADSTS50011: 'The redirect URI does not match the redirect URI configured for the app'
# Fix: Register the exact redirect_uri (including scheme, port, path) in the app.
#      Localhost variations like http://localhost:8080 and http://localhost:8080/ differ.

# AADSTS700016: 'Application was not found in the directory'
# Fix: The CLIENT_ID is wrong or the app is in a different tenant than TENANT_ID.
#      For multi-tenant apps, use 'common' as the tenant segment.

# AADSTS50058: 'A silent sign-in request was sent but no user is signed in'
# Fix: You used prompt=none but there is no active session. Add prompt=login or omit prompt.

# Token validation error: 'Audience validation failed'
# Fix: The 'aud' in the access token does not match your API's App ID URI.
#      Ensure clients request your API's scope, not Graph or another app.
#      Example correct scope: 'api://your-api-client-id/Data.Read'
XSS attack steals refresh token → full account access
Use sessionStorage or in-memory store; enable Continuous Access Evaluation
Tokens in logs can be replayed until expiry
Log only the first/last 8 chars or the token hash for correlation
Request minimum required scopes per flow
Secret leak gives persistent app access
Use certificates; rotate secrets on a schedule via Key Vault
Always validate signature, aud, iss, exp in every API endpoint

OAuth 2.0 is the authorization protocol underpinning every Entra ID API integration. Choose the Authorization Code + PKCE flow when a user signs in, Client Credentials for unattended daemon apps, and Device Code for CLIs. Validate every incoming access token in your API — never trust unsigned tokens.
Authorization Code + PKCE replaces implicit grant for all new SPAs and native apps
Client credentials tokens are app-only — no user is present and no user claims are included
Access tokens expire in 1 hour; use refresh tokens to renew silently without user interaction
Always validate JWT signatures, audience, issuer, and expiry in your API layer
Use MSAL or microsoft-identity-web rather than raw HTTP calls — they handle caching, refresh, and validation
